one_liner: A 62-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, and gum bleeding after brushing his teeth. He has also noted low-grade fevers and shortness of breath climbing stairs.

stage2.hpi_extension: Symptoms began insidiously about 3 weeks ago with fatigue, pallor, and dyspnea on exertion. He now notices spontaneous gingival bleeding and scattered bruises on both arms after minor bumps. He has had intermittent fevers to 38.3°C without localizing source. No night sweats or weight loss. He denies headache, vision changes, or focal neurologic deficits but reports mild bone pain in his sternum.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension. Prior chemotherapy (etoposide) for testicular cancer 7 years ago.
stage2.social_history: Retired carpenter. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: pale, ill-appearing but alert. HEENT: gingival hyperplasia with oozing at gumline, conjunctival pallor. Skin: scattered petechiae on lower extremities and ecchymoses on bilateral forearms. Lymph nodes: no significant lymphadenopathy. Abdomen: soft, mild splenomegaly, no hepatomegaly. Sternal tenderness to palpation. Neuro: nonfocal.

lab_results.result: WBC 42,000/µL (markedly elevated), Hgb 7.2 g/dL, platelets 24,000/µL. Differential: 68% blasts. Smear: large immature cells with high N:C ratio, prominent nucleoli, and needle-shaped azurophilic cytoplasmic inclusions (Auer rods). Rare myeloblasts contain bundles of Auer rods (faggot cells).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts and Auer rods is diagnostic of AML. Faggot cells (bundled Auer rods) are highly suggestive of acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL, AML-M3).

lab_results.result: Bone marrow biopsy: hypercellular marrow with >20% myeloblasts replacing normal hematopoietic elements. Blasts are myeloperoxidase (MPO)+, CD13+, CD33+. Flow cytometry confirms myeloid lineage. Extensive Auer rods present.
lab_results.clinical_significance: ≥20% blasts in bone marrow meets WHO criteria for AML. MPO positivity and Auer rods confirm myeloid lineage and distinguish AML from ALL.

lab_results.result: Cytogenetics: t(15;17)(q22;q12) — PML-RARα fusion identified by FISH and RT-PCR. Coagulation studies: PT prolonged, aPTT prolonged, fibrinogen low, D-dimer markedly elevated, consistent with DIC.
lab_results.clinical_significance: t(15;17) PML-RARα fusion is diagnostic of acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL, M3), which is frequently complicated by DIC. APL is treated with all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) and is now highly curable.

stage5.final_clue: The patient is started on ATRA plus arsenic trioxide with supportive transfusions for DIC. Within days, peripheral blasts fall dramatically and gingival bleeding resolves.

debrief.classic_presentation: AML classically affects adults (median age ~65) who present over weeks with symptoms of marrow failure: fatigue/pallor from anemia, infections from neutropenia, and bleeding/petechiae from thrombocytopenia. APL (M3 subtype) notably presents with DIC and oozing from mucosal surfaces. Prior chemotherapy (alkylators, topoisomerase II inhibitors like etoposide) or myelodysplastic syndrome increases risk (therapy-related AML).
debrief.pathophysiology: AML is a clonal proliferation of myeloid precursor cells (myeloblasts) that fail to differentiate and accumulate in the bone marrow, crowding out normal hematopoiesis. Diagnosis requires ≥20% blasts in marrow or blood. Auer rods are crystallized granules of myeloperoxidase pathognomonic for myeloid lineage. APL (M3) arises from t(15;17) PML-RARα fusion, which blocks retinoic acid receptor signaling and arrests cells at the promyelocyte stage; released granules activate the clotting cascade, causing DIC.
debrief.treatment_overview: Non-APL AML: induction with '7+3' (7 days cytarabine + 3 days anthracycline like daunorubicin), followed by consolidation ± allogeneic stem cell transplant for high-risk disease. APL: all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) forces promyelocytes to differentiate and resolves the DIC, combined with arsenic trioxide or anthracycline. ATRA has transformed APL from the most fatal AML subtype into the most curable (>90%).
high_yield_pearls: Diagnostic threshold for acute leukemia = ≥20% blasts in bone marrow or peripheral blood.
Auer rods (MPO-positive needle-like inclusions) are pathognomonic for AML, and bundles (faggot cells) are classic for APL (M3).
APL t(15;17) fuses PML with RARα; ATRA induces differentiation of arrested promyelocytes.
APL patients commonly present with DIC — bleeding/DIC risk increases during induction, treat promptly with ATRA.
Risk factors for AML: prior chemo (alkylators, etoposide), radiation, myelodysplastic syndrome, Down syndrome, benzene exposure.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Leukemias — Acute myelogenous leukemia (AML)' section.
